title: Self-organized Floquet band geometry in cavity-driven quantum materials
authors: Christopher Yang, Gil Refael, Mark S. Rudner, Iliya Esin
abstract: Floquet engineering has emerged as a powerful route to dynamically control band structure and topology in quantum materials, but most implementations rely on externally imposed laser fields that are power intensive, difficult to integrate into devices, and weakly coupled to the electronic system. We propose and analyze an alternative paradigm in which a self-generated cavity field Floquet-dresses the electronic bands and produces a geometric Hall response in an electrically driven cavity material system. We consider a semiconductor layer embedded in a cavity and coupled to external leads and a bath of acoustic phonons, where dc pumping leads to the buildup of a coherent intracavity field through light-matter coupling. We determine the resulting nonequilibrium steady state self-consistently and show that, above threshold, the coupled system settles into a stable time-periodic limit cycle with a field amplitude set by the cavity quality factor and dissipation. This emergent periodic field Floquet-dresses the electronic bands and modifies the anomalous Hall response of a material with broken time-reversal symmetry. We demonstrate that the resulting Hall conductivity can be directly probed via in-plane dc transport measurements. Our work establishes a route to self organized Floquet band reconstruction and geometric transport without external laser illumination, highlighting cavity driven steady states as a platform for electrically controlled nonequilibrium phases.
summary: The paper proposes using the self-generated intracavity field within an electrically pumped quantum material to achieve Floquet band engineering without external lasers. By analyzing the non-equilibrium steady state, it shows that this process creates a stable periodic drive that modifies electronic bands and results in a measurable anomalous Hall effect. This establishes a highly efficient platform for realizing topological phenomena using only DC bias.
main_problem: To develop an alternative method for Floquet engineering that uses self-generated cavity fields instead of high-power external lasers to dynamically control band structure and induce a geometric Hall response.
main_result: The system self-organizes into a stable time-periodic limit cycle above threshold, which 'Floquet-dresses' the bands and generates a measurable anomalous Hall conductivity via in-plane DC transport measurements.
method: Self-consistent determination of the non-equilibrium steady state by solving coupled rate equations for electronic occupations (using Floquet theory) and cavity photon number dynamics.
model_or_system: A semiconductor layer (TMD) embedded in an optical cavity, coupled to external leads and acoustic phonons. The system dynamics are governed by light-matter coupling leading to a self-consistent intracavity field buildup.
key_observables: Anomalous Hall conductivity ($\sigma_{xy}$), Floquet gap ($\Delta_F$), and the steady-state photon number ($n^*$).
important_parameters: Cavity quality factor, dissipation rates, DC bias voltage ($eV_z$), and the ratio of tunneling/recombination rates ($\Gamma/M$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes a TMD with broken time-reversal symmetry leading to polarization selection; it treats the cavity field as quasi-static in certain limits.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the schematic setup, the balance between photon gain and loss determining steady states, the opening of the Floquet gap ($\Delta F$) versus bias voltage, and the resulting photo-induced change in Hall conductivity ($\Delta\sigma_{xy}$).
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the physical problem (avoiding external lasers) to developing the self-consistent model using rate equations for occupations and photon number. It then analyzes the emergence of the periodic drive, the resulting band modification (Floquet dressing), and finally predicts measurable transport signatures like $\Delta\sigma_{xy}$.
why_interesting: This work directly bridges cavity QED principles with non-equilibrium condensed matter physics by showing how internal feedback mechanisms can generate coherent driving fields, which is a key theme in advanced AMO/quantum optics research.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Setup and main results. (a) Schematic of the pro- posed cavity material device. A TMD monolayer is embedded between p- and n-doped distributed Bragg reflectors (DBRs), which both form an optical cavity resonant at frequency ωc and serve as vertical electrical contacts biased by Vz. An in-plane probe current Ixy is driven through the TMD, and the transverse Hall voltage VH is measured to extract the Hall conductivity σxy. (b) Photon gain and loss as a function of the cavity photon occupation n, with the corresponding Floquet gap ∆F indicated on the upper axis. Solid curves show the electronic gain G(n) for several values of eVz slightly above ℏωc, while the dashed curve shows the...

FIG. 2. Induced Floquet spectrum. (a) Floquet quasienergy spectrum and scattering processes responsible for gain saturation. The biased top and bottom leads, with chem- ical potentials µT and µB, inject and remove carriers near the photon resonance εkν = ±ℏωc/2. Recombination, with rate M, and phonon relaxation, with rate R, compete with photon- assisted Floquet-Umklapp processes that change the cavity photon number by ±1 and occur with rate ˜R = R(∆F /ℏωc)2. (b) Floquet band structure showing the cavity-induced gap ∆F . (c) Steady-state gap ∆F as a function of the cavity quality factor Q and bias eVz = µT −µB.

FIG. 3. Phenomenological model. (a) Folded Floquet- band representation of the scattering processes shown in the extended-zone picture in Fig. 2(a). The patches So and Si denote states near the resonance ring in the upper Floquet band, while So′ and Si′ are their particle-hole counterparts in the lower band. Solid arrows indicate photon-assisted phonon processes that emit (+) or absorb (−) a cavity photon, with rate ˜R = R(∆F /ℏωc)2, and squiggly arrows indicate recom- bination processes with rate M. (b) Average occupations Fo and Fi as a function of the Floquet gap ∆F . (c) Steady-state gap ∆∗ F as a function of phonon scattering rate R and lead tunneling rate Γ, both normalized by the...

FIG. 4. Microscopic model and Hall response. (a) Band structure of a magnetically doped TMD with spin-orbit cou- pling. The top and bottom filtered leads, with chemical po- tentials µT and µB, create a nonequilibrium population. Due to spin-valley splitting, optical selection rules, Pauli blocking, and energy conservation, only one resonant spin-valley transi- tion contributes to gain, selecting a single circularly polarized cavity mode. (b) Berry curvature Bk+ and occupation fk+ of the upper Floquet band for a field amplitude below the self-consistent steady-state value, A < A∗, and at the steady state, A = A∗, where A∗denotes the steady-state cavity- field amplitude. (c) Photo-induced...

FIG. 5. Experimental signatures of the Floquet gap. (a) Out-of-plane current response following a bias quench. The cavity is initialized at bias Vi and rapidly quenched to Vf, while the field amplitude remains approximately fixed during the measurement. The plotted current is normalized by eΓlead, and the suppression of |Iz| near eVf ≃ℏωc sig- nals the Floquet gap. (b) Optical conductivity σxx(ωp) as a function of probe frequency ωp for different biases Vz. The colored arrows indicate the frequency window associated with the Floquet gap.
